dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 58-year-old male with breathing problems at a dialysis center in [CITY].
Hello, I'm Alex, EMT with the rescue squad. My partner is Sarah. How can we help you today?
You looked in some distress on arrival. What brought you to call 911 today?
I've been feeling very short of breath for about 3 days and wanted to get out of dialysis early.
Let's check your breathing and oxygen level. How has your shortness of breath been over the last few days?
It's been getting more noticeable since this morning. I feel like I can't take a deep breath.
Can you tell me your name and the date today, please?
I am Stan. I believe today is the 3rd of April 2019.
What city are we currently in? What happened today?
We are in [CITY]. I felt short of breath and left dialysis too early.
Thanks for staying with me. We're going to give you high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask now. Okay with that?
Starting oxygen at 10 liters per minute.
Okay, it's a little better once I got the mask.
May I put a pulse oximeter on your finger to check your oxygen? It won't hurt.
Pulse ox is 72% on 3 L via nasal cannula. Let's bump to 10 L on NRM now for higher support.
We've increased your oxygen flow. Let's now check your respiration rate.
Respirations are 12 breaths per minute initially. Let's monitor that closely.
Has your breathing ever felt this way before?
I've had it before, but not this severe on dialysis.
Initial BP is 163/113, heart rate at 97. Shall I start an IV line?
Please try an IV start in the arm or leg, if possible.
Lining up the IV. Unsuccessful first attempt. Shall I try another?
That's okay, one more try if needed.
Let's go over your medical history with you. Could you tell us more about the shortness of breath?
I felt more short of breath last week, but nothing this severe.
Have you had dizziness or chest tightness along with the breathing issue?
No chest pain, but I felt a bit lightheaded earlier today.
Do you have any allergies or medications you take regularly?
I take Hectorol, aspirin, and some for kidney health-can't remember all the names.
Got to know. Do you have any known medical conditions?
I have kidney disease. End stage. I've been on dialysis for a while now.
Your pain is at zero, but we will continue to monitor it throughout.
We found you awake and alert. You're aware of time, place, self, and person.
Your respiratory rate has increased to 24 breaths per minute now.
Second BP check: 158/99. Pulse was strong and regular.
Heart rate is 91 now, with a regular rhythm.
Oxygen saturation improved to 93% after the high-flow oxygen.
Glucose levels are out of range. No specimen was collected due to your renal condition.
Patching on the 4-lead ECG now. Let's monitor rhythm first.
EKG is done and shows no acute changes. Sinus rhythm present.
We will follow the pulmonary edema protocol now given your BP and oxygen response to CPAP.
Let's initiate CPAP. Attach the mask with gentle pressure, Sarah.
Mask is secured. Starting with 5 cm of pressure. Shall I increase it to 7.5 now?
Hold the current settings for now. How are you feeling with the CPAP, Stan?
It helps a little. I can feel more air coming in now.
BP is still 158/99 after five minutes. Increasing CPAP pressure now to 7.5 cm water.
Oxygen saturation is 92% after the CPAP adjustment. Heart rate and BP haven't changed.
Good, let's prepare to move you to the ambulance. CPAP is on and comfort level seems better.
Arrived at 7:26 with vitals: 91 pulse, 25 breaths, 93% oxygenation. Things are stable.
We need to transport you to [COMPANY_NAME] for further evaluation. Is that okay with you?
Yeah, I don't want to wait. Take me there.
We're ready to move, Sarah. Let's use the sheet lift and lower Stan gently onto the stretcher.
Got it. Ready to transfer.
Third check in between: BP is stable at 152/103, heart rate slightly at 89.
Oxygen now at 89%. No chest pain reported. CPAP provides some relief.
One final EKG before arrival. Made a few more data points as we were moving.
Still basement lead issues. Decided earlier not to administer furosemide due to end-stage renal disease.
You remain alert and oriented throughout the entire call. All four responses were intact.
Final BP check at 152/103, with 24 respirations and 89% saturation.
Glucose remains 0. No specimens collected due to your renal condition.
All right, Stan, we'll hand over your care here. Let's keep the CPAP on for the transition.
Now's the best I've felt all day. Just wish the hospital was closer.
